title: Lightweight Sessions
text: Lightweight Sessions Lightweight sessions allow multiple user sessions within a single database connection. This functionality is provided by Oracle through the Oracle Call Interface (OCI). Each lightweight session uniquely identifies a connected user (authenticated by a database password) without the overhead of a separate database connection. Lightweight sessions preserve the identity of the real user through the middle tier, which is authorized to act on behalf of the client by the Oracle administrator. To authorize an application server to proxy a client, use the GRANT CONNECT THROUGH clause of the ALTER USER statement in Oracle. With this clause you can also specify roles that the application server is permitted to activate when connecting as the client. Lightweight sessions can be specified via the assignment file as follows: $ PathName =ORA:{ Database } | { UserName }{ # ClientUsername } | { Password } Once a lightweight session path is identified, Oracle will: Check if a 'master' database connection (Database/UserName/Password) exists and is connected (if not, then a new "master" database connection is created). Create a lightweight session (ClientUsername) via the 'master' database connection. By default, master database connections are closed when all lightweight sessions are ended. To keep master connections open, even when paths are closed, use the disable lws close connector option. Master database connections and their lightweight sessions are always created via the OCI interface. By default, the first logon path to Oracle is created using the Pro*C/C++ precompiler interface, but if the first logon path is a lightweight session path then the OCI interface is used.
